text: Though I'm sharing a case of a sigmoid polyp, a distal sigmoid polyp, it is within a diverticulum. Our advanced gastroenterologist had made an attempt at getting it out and got the majority of it out, but part of it that was deep within the tick was still not accessible. So the two of us chose to do a combined laparoscopic-endoscopic procedure.

There was no dysplasia, it was just a tubular adenoma. He's showing me right here. That's the tick that the polyp is in. Sorry, I don't have the endoscopic pictures of it, but the polyp is right in there.

And the thought initially was if I could just invert this into the colon, that maybe he'd be able to just get a snare around it. And we tried this and I just wasn't able to get a good angle or to just press right on the spot well enough for him.
The surgeon uses cautery to access the bowel lumen and performs a wedge resection of the diverticulum on the anti-mesenteric surface of the colon, excising the area containing the polyp.

The surgeon holds the section of the colon containing the polyp in their left hand, while cutting across adjacent diverticula. The visual shows the excision of the bowel beneath the polyp, preparing for closure.

The surgeon visually identifies the polyp located beneath the left-hand instrument. The polyp is situated at the anti-mesenteric surface of the bowel wall, with minimal involvement of the lateral aspect where the mesentery joins, allowing for straightforward excision.

I think there are many ways of dealing with this type of problem. If the patient had had prior episodes of diverticulitis, I probably would have recommended a sigmoid resection. The patient has never had an episode of diverticulitis, And this was just found on a routine colonoscopy. And for that reason, I felt that it would have been beneficial for them for this benign polyp that just was inaccessible for surveillance or endoscopic removal to do what you see me doing here, preserving the sigmoid colon.

The specimen is separated, and the surgeon examines the defect using three 5mm ports. The gastroenterologist prepares to remove the specimen through the colon via a natural orifice. A distal diverticulum is visible, partially transected.

The surgeon divides a bridge of tissue to open it to the lumen of the colon, stabilizing the bowel with a 3-0 silk suture on the far lateral aspect. The defect is visible as a small opening on the left anterior aspect of the sigmoid colon, which is closed using a V-lock suture.

There are a lot of ways of dealing with these polyps. Once you have chosen to do a combined procedure, you absolutely could do this in the laparoscopic fashion like you see me doing here. We could have chosen to do a robotic. This case took less than an hour. I didn't think it was worth the setup of the robot for this, although the suturing certainly could have been easier.

But overall, I think that this type of procedure is very beneficial to a patient because they have complete knowledge that the polyp is dealt with. This was done as an outpatient procedure. We've preserved the sigmoid colon. Yes, they are at risk of future sigmoid diverticulitis, but as I've mentioned, they've never had a episode of diverticulitis in the past. you know, really minimal morbidity to a procedure like this.
The surgeon closes the defect on the bowel wall transversely to prevent narrowing of the lumen. A post-closure colonoscopy is performed to ensure the scope can pass above the closure.

The surgeon inserts a needle through a five millimeter trocar, causing a slight bend at the tip, but it does not affect the suturing of the bowel wall. The inner layer is sutured in an over-and-over fashion, and the surgeon proceeds to imbricate the bowel wall, similar to closing the common channel in an intracorporeal anastomosis.

I maintain that I believe that all of us as minimally invasive surgeons should be very comfortable with intracorporeal suturing. This type of procedure certainly wouldn't be possible if the surgeon was not comfortable doing this. I think that it's well within the skill set for all of us to be able to do it. You could make the argument that you could have just put a stapler across this. You could have just done a sigmoid resection. Lots of different ways. I think that the intracorporeal suturing that you're seeing here is a really nice way, and that's why I chose it.

The surgeon completes the closure of the bowel wall in two layers using silk sutures, tying the final stitch to the lateral stay suture. The surgical field shows the completed closure without any visible drains.

And now we'll go ahead and I'll perform a scope to ensure there's no bleeding, that it's patent and without luminal narrowing. Again, I'm sorry I don't have the endoscopic view of things for the video purposes, but you can trust that the lumen is widely patent. There's the scope going through with ease. The proximal colon did end up getting very distended and obscuring the view at the end of the procedure from all the gas insufflation, but no leak and patent, and I hope you enjoyed.
